transcript:
Hello, everyone. My name is Marcus, and I run Greenloop Ventures -- a cleantech VC fund based in Toronto.

I want to talk to you today about something that keeps me up at night. Not in a bad way -- in that restless, excited way you get when you know something important is shifting.

Here is the question: What if the greatest investment opportunity of the next decade is the one most investors are still too impatient to see?

Let me back up. Eighteen months ago, I sat across the table from a founder in Halifax. She had built a biochar system that turns agricultural waste into a stable carbon sink -- and simultaneously enriches soil so that crop yields go up. Beautiful technology. Rigorous science. Clear path to revenue.

But she had been turned down by fourteen venture firms. Fourteen. The feedback was always some version of the same thing: the payback period is too long, the market is too niche, come back when you have faster growth.

We wrote her a check. A seven hundred and fifty thousand dollar seed round. Today, eighteen months later, she has contracts with three provincial governments and a pilot with one of the largest agricultural co-ops in North America. Her revenue run rate has gone from zero to two point four million dollars.

So what happened? Did the market suddenly get bigger? Did the payback period shrink? No. The world caught up to where she already was.

This is the pattern I see over and over in climate tech. The founders are not early -- the capital is late.

At Greenloop, we have made twenty-two investments over the last four years. Seed to Series A, two hundred fifty thousand to two million dollar checks. We take board seats. We bring our network. And here is what I have learned: the companies solving real sustainability problems -- circular packaging, grid-scale storage, regenerative agriculture, industrial decarbonization -- they do not follow the hockey-stick growth curve that traditional venture expects. They follow what I call a root-system curve.

Think about how a forest grows. For years, the visible growth above ground is modest. But underground, the root network is expanding -- building resilience, creating symbiotic relationships, establishing the infrastructure for explosive growth. Then one season, everything accelerates. The canopy fills in. The ecosystem becomes self-sustaining.

Our best-performing portfolio companies look exactly like this. Year one, they are building regulatory relationships, running pilots, earning trust with enterprise customers who cannot afford to adopt unproven technology. Year two, three -- the pilots convert. The references compound. And by year four, they are growing faster than any SaaS company in our peer funds' portfolios.

But you have to be patient enough to invest in the root system.

Now, I know what some of you are thinking. Patient capital sounds lovely, Marcus, but LPs want returns. Fair point. So let me share some numbers.

Our Fund One, vintage twenty-twenty-two, is currently tracking at two point eight x net after four years. Our top quartile deals -- seven of twenty-two -- are tracking above five x. And here is the thing: not a single one of those top performers would have passed a traditional venture screen in their first twelve months. Every single one would have been flagged as too slow.

The venture model was built for software. Move fast, break things, winner-take-all dynamics. But the climate economy does not work like software. You cannot iterate a chemical plant the way you iterate a landing page. You cannot A-B test a power grid.

What you can do is back founders who understand the physics, the policy landscape, and the customer pain deeply enough to build something that endures. And when you do that -- when you match patient capital with extraordinary founders solving existential problems -- the returns are not just competitive. They are exceptional. Because you are investing in markets that are growing for structural reasons, not hype cycles.

So here is my challenge to this room -- to every investor, every founder, every person building in the startup ecosystem.

Stop asking climate founders to act like software companies. Start asking software investors why they are ignoring the largest market transition in human history.

The energy transition alone is a multi-trillion-dollar reallocation of global capital. Industrial decarbonization. Circular supply chains. Regenerative food systems. These are not niche markets. These are the economy of the next fifty years.

And the founders building these companies? They are right here. They are at events like this. They are in your city, in your accelerator, in your inbox. They just need investors who understand that the best returns -- like the best forests -- grow slowly at first.

At Greenloop, we have a saying: we do not chase unicorns. We grow sequoias.

Thank you.
